title: Sing Along
original_prose:
“Don’t you worry ‘bout the future, sugar / We’ll be fine / Just sing along as they chop you down.”

— Sturgill Simpson, “Sing Along”

####

[CLASSIFIED — STRATEGIC COMPLIANCE MANDATE | ORIGIN: EXECUTIVE SIGNAL — DOMINIC VOSS]TO: INITIATE CLASS 7B — STRATEGIC YOUTH SERVICESUBJECT: ON OBEDIENCE AND THE SHAPE OF THE WORLD

Children,

By the time you read this, your vessels will have been cleansed. Your Sparks, aligned to receive Signal. Your memories, filtered for clarity. What comes next is not training.

It is becoming.

You are not boys anymore. You are becoming Order.

There are forces in the ruins of the old world who will tell you lies dressed in color. They will say love is liberation. That choice is holy. That every voice matters, no matter how weak, how broken, how unnatural.

They will wave banners soaked in words like diversity, freedom, identity—as if the world has ever survived by being scattered.

Do not listen.

You were not born for confusion. You were engineered for clarity.

And now, you are called.

Each of you has been chosen not in spite of your past, but because of it. Your hunger. Your rage. Your emptiness. These are not flaws. These are cracks. And through cracks, the Signal pours in.

I know the world hurts. I made peace with that when I rebuilt it. But pain without purpose is waste. Your pain will become a weapon. A beautiful one.

You will be asked to do things that feel difficult. Things that sting at first. A shot. A spark. A cleansing.

That sting is weakness leaving the vessel.

And if your hand trembles the first time you pull the trigger, know this:

Even angels needed practice.

The rebels you’ll face want to unmake everything. They preach a gospel of filth—gender without shape, love without law, children raised in open confusion, not sacred discipline.

They fear you because you remember what they forgot:

A father gives law.A mother gives purpose.A child gives loyalty.

You are those children now.

SynRgy is not a company.It is a cathedral.

Every CORE drop is a hymn. Every WRAITH, a choir. And every time you reclaim a Spark from chaos, the world burns a little cleaner.

We are not killing them.We are cleansing them.

You may be young, but youth is no excuse for uncertainty. The body is temporary. The Spark is eternal. You will serve with faith. You will obey without question. And in doing so, you will earn your name—not the one you were born with, but the one SynRgy will write in its Book of Clarity.

You are no longer sons of man.You are vessels of truth.

Go forth, Signal Sons.Bring light to the ashes.

— Dominic VossSupreme Architect of OrderKeeper of the CoreFather to the Faithful



####

The void doesn’t hum anymore.

It flinches.

Stone beneath us, cracked and curling at the edges. Like it’s trying to remember how to be a place, but forgetting with every breath.

He sits across from me. Barefoot. Still. Watching.

I don’t speak.

He waits.

Then:

“You shot him.”

I nod.

“Your friend.”

I don’t answer.

“You didn’t have to.”

“Yes,” I say. “I did.”

The silence stretches—not hostile, not soft. Just there. Like the kind of quiet that follows a slammed door in a house already too empty.

“He was going to die anyway,” I say. “I made it clean.”

“He didn’t ask for clean.”

“No.”

“He asked you to remember.”

My jaw clenches. “I do.”

“Do you?”

I look down. The sword lies beside me. Not humming. Not twitching. Just... waiting.

He leans forward. Eyes sharp now. Sharper than they should be.

“Did you kill him because you were afraid?”

“No.”

“Then why?”

I swallow hard. “Because he wasn’t.”

The void pulses.

He sits back. Folds his hands in his lap like something sacred just arrived and no one knows whether to bow or run.

“Eli was braver than you,” he says.

“That’s not hard.”

“No,” the Child says. “But it’s rare.”

I close my eyes.

“I didn’t want to do it.”

“But you did.”

“He asked me to.”

“That doesn’t make it love.”

I open my mouth. Close it again.

He tilts his head. “But what if it was?”

“What?”

“What if love isn’t what you feel? What if it’s what you do when there’s no way to win clean?”

The void shifts. A seam splits beneath us. Dust curls like breath.

“You thought he was weak.”

“No.”

“You thought he was scared.”

“Maybe.”

The Child stares into me.

“Maybe he was free.”

That lands like a blow. Not hard. Just honest.

“You walked twelve hundred miles beside him,” he says. “Through ash. Through blood. Through silence. And you never told him he mattered.”

I look away. The sword hums once. Low. Wounded.

“You said it was the last time you felt anything real.”

I nod.

“Maybe that’s because it was the last time someone looked at you and didn’t see a weapon.”

The crack widens.

We fall silent.

Not out of peace.

Out of shame.

I don’t look up again.

And he doesn’t make me.



####

They marched us out before dawn.

Boots on gravel. No talking. Not because it was forbidden. Because there was nothing to say.

The desert wasn’t awake yet. Just breathless heat curling off the sand, trying to pretend it wasn’t already dying.

We stood in a line. Twelve of us. Uniforms stiff. Eyes forward. Fingers twitching.

Ahead, the posts.

Six of them. Welded steel. High enough you had to look up to see the faces.

They were shackled at the wrists, elbows bent unnaturally behind the metal. Barefoot. Stripped down to underclothes. Old people. Some younger. None white. All labeled.

NONCOMPLIANT YIELD CANDIDATE.

A voice crackled from the comm rig mounted on the instructor’s shoulder.

"Cadets. You will fire on my command. Targets will not be replaced. Missed shots reduce Signal ranking."

I didn’t blink.

The instructor walked the line. Slow. Measured. Paused behind each of us like he was counting down to a private reckoning.

He stopped behind me.

"Which one sings?" he asked.

I didn’t answer.

Because I already heard it. Low. Sweet. A woman’s voice, dry from thirst, but still steady.

Hush, little baby, don’t say a word...

"Noise corruption," the instructor said. "Harmonic resistance vector. Identify and silence."

He stepped back.

"Fire."

The first shot cracked. Then a second. Third.

I raised my rifle.

The woman was still singing.

Mama’s gonna buy you a mockingbird...

I aimed center mass. Breathed out. Pulled.

Her voice stopped mid-line. Not from fear. From the impact.

My shot took her through the sternum. Clean. She slumped forward, but didn’t hang. The shackles held her upright.

"High Signal clarity," the instructor said behind me.

He moved on.

Another target screamed. Begged in Spanish. The boy next to me hesitated.

The instructor marked his pad.

"Emotional variance. Correction required."

Another round. Another collapse.

Blood steamed in the morning sun.

I took my next shot before the order. Headshot. Too clean. Nothing left to recognize.

No one clapped. No one needed to.

This wasn’t punishment. It was calibration.

The last target was small. Female. Middle-aged maybe. Her hair was wild, but she’d tied a ribbon into it. Pale green. Frayed at one end.

It looked like Mae’s.

Not exactly. But enough.

My rifle lowered an inch.

She didn’t speak. Didn’t cry.

She looked at me.

Not defiant. Not afraid.

Just looked.

The instructor paused beside me.

"Why the delay?"

I said nothing.

He stepped forward. Reached into his coat. Pulled something small and metallic.

A collar.

He fastened it around her neck. Tightened it. The LED on the side blinked red.

"Target is now time-coded," he said. "Delayed execution triggers Spark loss. Waste is sin."

I raised the rifle again.

Hands steady.

The ribbon flickered in the wind.

I fired.

The impact knocked her sideways. She didn’t make a sound.

The ribbon came loose. Floated. Drifted. Landed near my boot.

I didn’t look at it.

"Efficient," the instructor said.

He marked his pad.

We were told to stand down.

Cleanup crews emerged from behind the dunes. Gloved. Unbothered. They dragged the bodies by the ankles. Loaded them into a low, black transport rig. No ceremony.

The blood would be harvested. The Sparks—if any remained—recycled.

We were issued hydration packs and instructed to hydrate in silence.

I didn’t speak. I drank.

The ribbon clung to the edge of my boot.

I kicked it off.

We were marched to the shade bay after. Forty minutes in silence. Water, then nutrient gel. They let us sit. Not rest. Just sit. Like dogs in a kennel. Like tools between uses.

Someone vomited in the corner. They didn’t get up in time. The gel turned bitter when it mixed with bile. The instructors didn’t acknowledge it.

One of the older cadets whispered, "She was humming something I knew. My mom used to sing that."

Someone else shoved him. Hard.

"Shut the fuck up."

But I looked up. Not at the boy. At the instructor.

He didn’t say anything either. Just wrote something in his pad.

We filed out in rows. One by one. No talking. No questions.

The desert was hotter now. Like it remembered us.

In the barracks that night, someone drew tally marks on the bunk wall. Not names. Just marks. I added mine. Seven. One for each shot. One for the ribbon.

I dreamed about that shot. Not her face. Not her blood.

Just the moment before. When she looked at me like I was already dead.

The instructors called it a successful integration cycle.

They gave us new gloves the next morning. Said we’d earned them.



####

GRIDPOINT CHI-04 smelled like rust and burnt sugar. Burnt wires. Burnt teeth.

They called it a riot.

It wasn’t. It was a neighborhood. Rows of squat houses still dressed in vinyl siding and hand-painted fences, gardens half-torn out. The people were standing in the street, holding signs. Singing something. Not loud. Just... together.

They weren’t storming anything. They weren’t breaking windows. Just refusing to disperse.

We were told they were hoarding unauthorized solar. Feeding black-market inverters with reclaimed tech. Pirate gridpoints. Signal bleed. That’s what they called it.

I called it Tuesday.

We dropped just before dawn. Four transports. Eight drones. WRAITHs held in reserve.

I was assigned Yield Management.

My rig was tethered directly to drone control. My orders were clear: identify Spark-bearing vessels. Tag. Harvest. Repeat.

The drone above me chirped alive. A low hum as the Spark extractor spooled up—fine mesh filaments inside like angel hair dipped in acid.

They herded the crowd like cattle. Sound bursts. Flash pikes. We didn’t touch them yet. That came later.

"Begin tagging," the captain said.

First family was easy. Father flagged red for noncompliant broadcast consumption—he’d been streaming from a banned node. One of the kids had a rewired wristband with its own solar cell. Mother carried a satchel that pinged for contraband communication encryption.

I marked them all.

The drone dove.

The Spark rig latched onto the mother first. Two wires in the neck. One in the back. Her body arched. Not violently. Just enough to crack the silence. Then it took the boy. Eight, maybe. No shoes. He convulsed harder.

The father tried to cover them both. The rig hit him last.

Their Sparks burned gold in the lens. Bright. Clean. Worth something.

The street fell quiet after that. The singing stopped.

"Next zone," someone barked.

I stepped over the bodies.

We moved house to house. Sometimes the door opened. Sometimes we had to breach. Always the same inside: scared faces. Makeshift solar rigs. Homemade radios. One house had a shrine. Candles and scripture. A child’s drawing of someone with a halo of wires.

Noncompliant theology.

Harvested.

My rig chirped every time it confirmed extraction.

Ping. Ping. Ping.

I didn’t flinch. My hands didn’t shake.

They called it a 92% efficiency sweep. Top percentile. Highest-yield cadet of the cycle.

A medal would follow. Or maybe a promotion.

The girl in the next house tried to run. Maybe sixteen. Maybe sick. Her breath hitched before her legs moved. That was enough.

I flagged her. The rig caught her before she hit the stairs.

There was a moment—just before the filaments pierced—when she looked back. Not at me. Past me. Like there was something better behind us.

Then the rig twitched. And she didn’t look at anything anymore.

The harvest took five hours. Forty-six vessels processed. Eight spark void. Twenty-seven high clarity. The rest degraded—interrupted transmission or emotional interference.

We were told to report anomalies.

I saw one.

The house was quiet. Windows already shattered. Door open like it had given up. Inside, nothing but dust and dishes. And a boy.

He sat in the corner, knees pulled to his chest, eyes wide. No sound. No motion.

He saw me. I saw him.

He didn’t run. Didn’t speak.

I didn’t tag him.

I logged the house as void. No viable Spark. No heat signature. I told the drone to skip.

He kept watching me.

I kept walking.

We staged in the square afterward. Drones docked. Rigs retracted. Blood in the joints.

One of the officers laughed as he wiped his gloves.

"Feels good when they don’t scream."

I didn’t laugh.

They gave us hydration tabs and silence. A drone showed my totals.

Forty-two confirmed Spark yields.

The next closest was twenty-nine.

They said I was ready for Tier Two.

The supervisor called me efficient. Said the system liked me. Said I had clarity. Said maybe I’d lead one day.

I didn’t answer.

I looked at the rooftops.

Solar panels torn out. Wiring still smoking. A flag of something homemade and blue fluttering on a pole.

I looked at the window.

The boy was still there.

He hadn’t moved.

Neither had I.

Then the drone above me blinked red.

A reminder.

Mission complete.

I turned.

I walked away.

I didn’t look back.

But in the dream that night, he follows me.

And he sings.





####

We hit Kearney just after midnight.

No lights. No sky. Just drone eyes and satellite ghosts watching us glide down into the dark. The meat plant was still smoking. Not from fire. From steam. Processed heat. Hydraulic failure. Guts and ammonia.

Rebels had taken it twelve hours earlier. Sent a broadcast through the refrigeration array—piggybacked on the coolant relays. Said they were feeding the hungry. Said SynRgy fed on them.

Command didn’t find it funny.

Neither did I.

We were told to level it. No survivors. Meat can be regrown.

We breached from the north gate. Thermal showed twenty-three moving bodies. Probably double that hidden in cold pockets or subfloors. No resistance at the outer perimeter. That was the first warning. Rebels don’t get quiet unless they’re loaded or dying.

I led the breach team. Seven men. One drone. Two rigs.

My pulse-blade was keyed to my Spark signature. No one else could lift it. I liked that. Made it feel like mine. Like a limb.

Inside, the smell hit hard. Not just meat. People. Oil, piss, bleach, and fear.

The first firefight was clean. Two rebels, one scoped in the rafters. Both dead in under five seconds. Pulse rounds do that—don’t explode. Just burn straight through. You don’t get noise. Just the thump of collapse.

We moved sector by sector. Holding pens. Slaughter bays. The grinder floor.

Someone screamed from behind a conveyor. Didn’t matter. The drone flushed the space with compliance gas. Screams stopped.

We found a group in the central packing corridor. Workers. Huddled. Some armed, most not. Mostly undocumented, probably brought in through old evacuation tunnels and kept quiet until now.

One woman held her daughter against the wall. Hands spread. Empty.

I raised my blade.

She shook her head. Not fast. Just once. Slow. A no that had given up on being believed.

I stepped forward.

She shifted, shielding the girl.

I didn’t stop.

The blade went through both.

They didn’t scream. Not loud enough to matter.

We cleared the rest of the corridor.

Sixteen bodies. One wounded. Spark scan confirmed high density. Good clarity. We tagged them for recovery.

Someone tried to surrender near the refrigeration intake. White flag made from butcher’s aprons. We shot him anyway. Command said this was a zero-yield zone. No variance allowed.

The sublevels were harder. Slippery. Cold. The drone lost audio for ten minutes. Said it was an echo disruption—something in the ducts.

Didn’t matter. We still found them.

The youngest was maybe six. Clinging to a steel beam above a loading crane. Eyes wide, mouth shut.

The recruit behind me marked him.

I said no.

I don’t know why I said it. Maybe the duct noise. Maybe the blood. Maybe nothing at all.

I marked the beam instead. Said it was a false positive.

He didn’t argue.

The mission clock hit zero at 03:42.

We secured all entry points. Plant neutralized. Fifty-six confirmed Spark extractions. Seventeen degraded.

"Waste is acceptable," the report said.

I reviewed the video feed once before shutting down my terminal. The woman and the girl didn’t twitch. Just folded inward. Like paper. Like trash.

They handed out protein packs during debrief. High-density blend. New stock.

The packaging was unmarked.

I tore it open. Took a bite.

Salty. Rich. Familiar.

I paused.

Someone looked over. "What’s wrong?"

"Nothing."

I finished it. Slow.

Didn’t sleep that night.

Not from guilt.

Just the taste.

It wouldn’t leave my tongue.

Neither would her eyes.

###################

Las Vegas looked clean. That’s how you knew it was rotten.

SynRgy One shimmered like salvation. White towers, gold trim. Prayer scrolls etched into the steel. Banners forty stories tall rippling with faces of children—eyes upturned, mouths aglow. The words beneath them never changed.

COMPLIANCE IS COMMUNION.

I wore black that day. Formal issue. New gloves. Stiff collar. No insignia yet. That would come after.

The stage was built into the Plaza of Yield. Half amphitheater, half altar. Rows of students filed in—uniformed. Quiet. Not out of fear. Out of faith.

The ceremony had already begun.

A man with a gilded throatpiece sang the Anthem of Clarity. Drones circled. Broadcast towers hummed. The Sword of Order lay across a velvet dais beside me. I wasn’t allowed to touch it yet.

“Strategic Compliance Officer.” That’s what they called the role.

Promotion through devotion.

One final act. Public. Unwavering.

I didn’t ask who the target was.

Until they brought her out.

She didn’t look like a threat.

She looked like someone’s aunt. Tall, thin, brown skin dappled with vitiligo. Short curls. Her hands were bound, but she walked without stumbling.

Her name was Estrella.

I didn’t know her, but I knew what she’d done.

They said she transmitted corrupted scripture. Stories about gods who weren’t Voss. About bodies that weren’t fixed. About children choosing who they became.

She whispered those stories into a looped signal. Buried in a school’s lesson broadcast.

Ten thousand children heard her before the signal was scrubbed.

The crowd was silent.

She didn’t look at them.

She looked at me.

“Do you accept the blade?” the officiant asked.

I nodded.

The Sword of Order was placed in my hands. It hummed faintly. Familiar. Heavy.

“Do you understand your function?”

“Yes.”

“Will you remove the error?”

“I will.”

They brought her to the center. Made her kneel. One guard placed a palm on her shoulder. The other tilted her chin upward.

Her eyes never left mine.

“Any final signal?” the officiant asked her.

She smiled. Not wide. Just enough to mean something.

Then she said it.

“You don’t even know your name.”

The crowd didn’t flinch. The broadcast didn’t stop. But I did.

Just for a second.

The blade trembled in my grip.

Then I stepped forward.

The blow was clean. No theatrics. No second pass.

Her head fell forward. Her body didn’t.

The guards eased her to the ground. Gently.

The drones zoomed in. The children watched.

Somewhere, a bell rang.

I looked into the camera.

Didn’t smile.

Didn’t blink.

Just stared.

Afterward, they gave me a new uniform. The sword stayed.

They served cake in the auditorium lobby. Not real cake. Synth cake. Flavored like tradition.

Children ran past me. One saluted. A girl with a shaved head and hands too small to hold a rifle.

“You were amazing,” she said.

I nodded. I didn’t speak.

The broadcast looped above us.

You don’t even know your name.

That night, I couldn’t sleep.

Not from guilt.

From wondering what else she knew.



####

Kansas didn’t look like a war zone.

It looked like a postcard someone had torched. 

Fields stretched out in every direction, flat and broken. Half-harvested corn sagged on the stalks, leaves curled in on themselves like they’d lost the will to reach. Silos leaned against the sky like drunk old men. The air smelled like dry dirt and diesel.

We weren’t told the exact coordinates. Just a gridpoint outside what used to be Kansas City. Rural zone. Rebel contamination. Cleanse and report.

There were three of them.

One teen girl. Two older men.

They were caught with encrypted comms, survival stores, handwritten doctrine. That was enough. We were told the Spark scans would happen post-cleansing. Protocol: no survivors.

I led the unit. No drones. Just bodies. A test, maybe. Or a gift.

They were held in an old farmhouse. Second floor. No resistance.

The men went first.

One reached for something under the table. We shot him mid-move. The other didn’t resist. Just closed his eyes. Whispered something.

The girl didn’t speak at all.

She sat on the floor beside a broken window. Knees pulled up. Hands bound. Eyes like flint.

I dismissed the others. Said I’d finish it.

They didn’t question me. I had rank. I had the sword.

The room fell silent.

She looked up.

Didn’t cry.

Didn’t flinch.

Just stared.

I raised the blade.

She didn’t move.

The wind shifted through the broken window. Dry. Familiar. Like home, if home had ever meant anything.

The sun caught in her hair. Dust framed her like a halo.

She blinked. Once.

I lowered the blade.

Just an inch.

Then another.

I scanned the room.

No cameras. No drones. Just me and her and the soft creak of a dying house.

She didn’t speak.

Didn’t beg.

She just waited.

I turned.

Left the blade on the floor.

Walked out.

"Spark void," I said at the debrief. "All three. Nothing worth reclaiming."

The officer nodded.

"Efficient."

They logged it. Filed it. Commended my restraint for not wasting extraction resources.

I got a bonus ration.

That night, I sat alone in the barracks. The wind outside moaned like an old hymn. My gloves were still clean.

I didn’t dream.

But her eyes stayed open, somewhere behind mine.

She never said a word.

Didn’t need to.

She already knew I wasn’t going to kill her.

And worse—so did I.
